focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 12

Dell woke her by swallowing the cry instead of letting it out.

Cora had slept crooked in the cane chair with her forearm across the quilt, feeling for him even in sleep the way she once used to feel for a calf in a hard birth, counting on touch more than sight. Before dawn the room was the color of old tin. The lamp had burned low enough to crust the chimney with soot. Dell lay in her bed on top of the sheet because he could not bear weight over his ribs, his left arm splinted between two shaved laths from a peach crate, wrapped in torn pillowcase strips already going gray from sweat and dust. He had managed to turn half an inch onto his right side, and that half inch had turned his face white under the fever.

He kept his teeth shut. That was how she knew it was bad.

She was up before he asked. The enamel basin waited under the chair with a rag folded in the bottom to keep it from clanging. She got it under his chin just as his breath hitched loose. He retched thin yellow bitter into it and shook afterward till the bed ropes hummed like thresher belts.

"Easy," she said. "Get it up."

"I'm not a horse."

"No. Horses are better tempered."

The answer cost him a look. His right eye opened on her, mean and glazed together. Sweat had pasted his dust-light hair to his forehead in little hooks. There was a cut in the scalp above his ear, not deep but ugly with dried blood, and the bruising along his right side had gone from dirt-brown to the color of smashed plums. Hester had bound the arm straight the evening before, jaw set, cough trapped in her chest like she would rather crack herself than let the boys from town see it hurt. Cora had thought the splint looked too rough then. In the night it had become precious, the only thing holding bone from shifting ag...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ade.

When the yard had mostly emptied, Hester went inside and washed her hands at the back sink. The rag came away from her mouth pink again when she checked. More than morning. Less than enough to stop her. She folded the rag tight and tucked it under the flour bin where nobody would find it tonight.

Outside, she could hear Dell testing the wagon hitch one last time and Thea reading names back off the ledger so nothing would be forgotten by dawn. Cora answered in that spare practical voice of hers, all verbs and no sentiment. It sounded almost like any other evening's work if a person did not listen for the strain under it.

Hester dried her hands and stood a moment in the kitchen doorway. The alley lay in long dust-colored shadow. Beyond the roofs, south, the land flattened toward Saint's Basin. You could not see the place from here. Only the idea of where it waited. Ground holding its own counsel under a skin of baked dirt and lies.

She thought of Otto's boots by the stove, black with basin mud. She thought of Marta's lungs and the thin red line in the basin this morning. She thought of August smoothing his jacket while he threatened hunger with polite nouns. She thought of Thea too, neat as a school copybook until she stepped into the alley and put her name down where everybody could read it.

In a town this small, a coalition began before the numbers did. It started with what could be seen from the street: a shovel lent, a name written, a woman crossing one stretch of road at the wrong time of day and not going back when warned.

By dark, every house in Morrow would know whose tools were in Cora Vale's wagon. By dawn, the town would know which mattered more to it: August's caution, or the chance to put iron into the old wound and see whether it still bled water.
